djm03178's blog

Codeforces Round #578 (Div. 2) Editorial
By djm03178, history, 7 years ago,

The problems with Gildong (B, D, F) are by me, and Amugae (A, C, E) are by nong.

1200A - Отельер

Make an array of size 10 filled with 0. Then for each character from the input: L : Find the first position containing 0, then change it to 1. R : Find the last position containing 0, then change it to 1. 0   9 : array[x] = 0.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n)

1200B - Block Adventure

We can easily see that it's always optimal to have as many blocks as possible in the bag before getting to the next column. Therefore, if the character is currently on the top of the ii-th column, Gildong just needs to make hihi become max(0,hi+1−k)max(0,hi+1−k) by repeating the 11-st or the 22-nd action. In other words, we should add hi−max(0,hi+1−k)hi−max(0,hi+1−k) blocks to the bag. Adding or subtracting one by one will lead to TLE. If there exists a situation where the bag will have negative number of blocks, the answer is NO. Otherwise the answer is YES.

Time complexity: O(n)O(n) for each test case.

1200C - Круглый коридор

Denote the corridor's length as 11. Then, there is a wall at (1,1n),(1,2n),⋯,(1,nn),(2,1m),(2,2m),⋯(2,mm)(1,1n),(1,2n),⋯,(1,nn),(2,1m),(2,2m),⋯(2,mm). For some value xx, If there are walls at (1,x)(1,x) and (2,x)(2,x) at the same time, we can't move from yy to zz for y<xy<x and z>xz>x. Let's call them a "dual wall."

Suppose g=gcd(n,m)g=gcd(n,m). Then dual walls exist at 1g,2g,⋯,gg1g,2g,⋯,gg. So we can make gg groups. We can move freely in the same group, and we can't move from one group to another group.For x=1x=1, (1,1),(1,2),⋯,(1,ng)(1,1),(1,2),⋯,(1,ng) belong to group 11, and (1,ng+1),(1,ng+2),⋯,(1,2ng)(1,ng+1),(1,ng+2),⋯,(1,2ng) belong to group 22, and so on. For x=2x=2, (2,1),(2,2),⋯,(2,mg)(2,1),(2,2),⋯,(2,mg) belong to group 11, and (2,mg+1),(2,mg+2),⋯,(2,2mg)(2,mg+1),(2,mg+2),⋯,(2,2mg) belong to group 22, and so on.

For each query, print YES if (sx,sy)(sx,sy) and (ex,ey)(ex,ey) belong to the same group. Otherwise, print NO.

time complexity: O(log(max(n,m))+q)O(log(max(n,m))+q)

1200D - White Lines

Let's consider a single row that contains at least one black cell. If the first appearance of a black cell is at the ll-th column and the last appearance of a black cell is at the rr-th column, we can determine whether it becomes a white line when a certain cell (i,j)(i,j) is clicked in O(1)O(1), after some preprocessing. It becomes a white line if and only if a cell (i,j)(i,j) is clicked where the row is at [i,i+k−1][i,i+k−1] and j≤l≤r≤j+k−1j≤l≤r≤j+k−1. We just need to compute ll and rr in advance.

Now let's consider all nn rows (not columns). First, count all rows that are already white lines before clicking. Then we count the number of white rows when the cell (1,1)(1,1) is clicked, by applying the above method to all rows from 11 to kk. Ignore the already-white rows that we counted before. So far we obtained the number of white rows when the cell (1,1)(1,1) is clicked. From now, we slide the window. Add the k+1k+1-st row and remove the 11-st row by applying the same method to them, and we obtain the number of white rows when the cell (2,1)(2,1) is clicked. We can repeat this until we calculate all n−k+1n−k+1 cases for clicking the cells at the 11-st column. Then we repeat the whole process for all n−k+1n−k+1 columns.

The same process can be done for counting white columns, too. Now we know the number of white rows and white columns when each cell is clicked, so we can find the maximum value among their sums.

Time complexity: O(n2)O(n2)

1200E - Compress Words

Denote the words from left to right as W1,W2,W3,⋯,WnW1,W2,W3,⋯,Wn.

If we define string F(k)F(k) as the result of merging as described in the problem kk times, we can get F(k+1)F(k+1) by the following process:

  1. If length of F(k)F(k) > length of Wk+1Wk+1
    • Assume the length of F(K)F(K) is xx, and the length of Wk+1Wk+1 is yy. Construct the string c=Wk+1+F(k)[x−y...x]c=Wk+1+F(k)[x−y...x] ( * s[x..y]s[x..y] for string ss is the substring from index xx to yy)
    • Get the KMP failure function from string cc.
    • We can get maximum overlapped length of Wk+1Wk+1's prefix and F(k)F(k)'s suffix from this function. Suppose the last element of the failure function smaller than the length of Wk+1Wk+1 is zz. Then the longest overlapped length of F(k)F(k)'s suffix and Wk+1Wk+1's prefix is min(z,y)min(z,y). Let L=min(z,y)L=min(z,y).
    • Then, F(k+1)=F(k)+Wk+1[L+1...y]F(k+1)=F(k)+Wk+1[L+1...y]
  2. Otherwise
    • Construct cc as Wk+1[1...x]+F(k)Wk+1[1...x]+F(k). We can get F(k+1)F(k+1) from the same process described in 1.

In this process, we can get F(k+1)F(k+1) from F(k)F(k) in time complexity O(len(Wk+1))O(len(Wk+1)). So, we can get F(N)F(N) (the answer of this problem) in O(len(W1)+len(W2)+⋯+len(WN))O(len(W1)+len(W2)+⋯+len(WN)).

1200F - Graph Traveler

Since a travel will never end, it is clear that every travel will eventually get into an infinite loop. But we should consider more than just the vertices, since cc could be different every time he visits the same vertex. Since the number of outgoing edges of each vertex is at most 1010, we can see a state can be reduced to lcm(1..10)=2520lcm(1..10)=2520 for each vertex. Therefore, we can think that the graph actually has 2520⋅n2520⋅n vertices, each with a single outgoing edge.

To simulate the travels, we just need to follow the exact process written in the description, except that cc should be kept in modulo 25202520. The problem is when to stop, and how to count the number of distinct vertices that are in the loop.

We can stop simulating until we find a state that we already have visited. There can be two cases when we find a visited state.

The first case is when we have not visited this state in the previous travels, i.e. this is the first travel that visits this state. We need to check all of the states after the first visit of this state and count the number of distinct vertices. Duplicated vertices can be removed simply by using a set, or more efficiently, using timestamp. Then we can apply the answer to all of the states we visited in this travel.

The second case is when the state was visited in one of the previous travels. We know that both the previous travel and the current travel will end in the same loop, so we can apply the same answer to all of the states we visited in this travel.

On a side note, the simulation can be done with recursion, but this can lead to maximum of 25200002520000 recursion depth. This causes stack overflow or recursion limit excess for some languages (including Java).

Time complexity: O(2520n+q)O(2520n+q)

Tutorial of Codeforces Round 578 (Div. 2)

  • djm03178
  • 7 years ago

+14

I wrote D different way. Consider each row. If it can be made white, max — min + 1 indices for black dots are <= k. Then we know the rectangle that, if we pick any point in it, will give us +1 to answer (that row will be white) We cannot add 1 to all points of it though, that is O(k^2) operation, so we mark starts with +1 and end points with -1 for each row. That makes it O(k). We do same for columns and pick biggest value.

    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +1

    ceil is prone to error. Better write your own, like you said, or maybe implement something along these lines:


    #define ll long long

ll ceil(const ll &a, const ll &b)
{
    if(a%b == 0)
        return a/b;
    return a/b +1;
}

    Instead of using ceil I just switched to zero-indexed (subtract 1 from y) and use the default truncated integer division. Since the only thing we want to do with the group index is to compare equality, there is no need to convert back to one-indexed.

Could somebody explain this code? https://codeforces.com/contest/1200/submission/58594933

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +23

    For prefix function one usually uses a string formed by:

    S=pattern+$+textS=pattern+$+text

    Where $ is a symbol that doesn't belong to the alphabet just to be sure that we won't be getting an invalid prefix value of patternpattern. Here we go with some observations:

    1) Since π[i]π[i] is the length of a prefix of patternpattern, then π[i]≤|pattern|π[i]≤|pattern|.

    2) In the usual preprocess, we initialize j=π[i−1]j=π[i−1], but this can be dismissed by using jj as an outside variable and recycling it for the next iteration.

    3) Combining 1 and 2, we notice that we just need to compute π[i]π[i] for 0≤i<|pattern|0≤i<|pattern| to get any π[i]π[i] needed afterwards (it doesn't even depend on the value of texttext :D).

    4) Since we already have all that we need to compute any π[i]π[i] without any extra data, we don't need to create SS as it is, just use a similar preprocess iteration over texttext and take the last jj value (since that would be π[|text|−1]π[|text|−1], exactly what we need).

    The rest is just the implementation required to solve the problem and some optimizations (like just taking the last min(|ans|,|word|)min(|ans|,|word|) characters of ansans).

    → Reply

n = int(input())
words = list(input().split())
left = list(words[0])
for i in range(1, len(words)):
    RP = 0;
    for j in range(min(len(left), len(words[i]))-1, -1, -1):
        LP = len(left)-1-j;
        if (left[LP] == words[i][RP]):
            RP += 1
        elif (left[LP] == words[i][0]):
            RP = 1
        else:
            RP = 0
    left.extend(words[i][RP:])
print("".join(left))

this is my submitted solution for E. I thought this approach is right, but it turned out to be not. could any one tell me why it is wrong? :D LP and RP are positions for left words and right words(indices for comparison) respectively

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    mkagenius
    7 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    mkagenius
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    Some points why this doesnt work:

    • You are incrementing LP and never looking back
    • When your match fails, LP never goes to original LP+1, it continues from where it failed.
    • You are missing to check all potential candidates which might have matched.
    → Reply

Are the tests on E too weak or is there some "good" upper bound on the depth of links in suffix automata? My submission:

https://codeforces.com/contest/1200/submission/58614095

The 998ms is quite close to the time limit, but honestly I expected this will simply TL.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    MathAlex
    7 years ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    MathAlex
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    This part of your code works in linear time of current string's length, because you mark all suffixes of string:

    int term = last;
while (term > 0)
    terminal[term] = 1, term = st[term].link;

    Total complexity is O(W2)O(W2), where WW is sum of strings length. So, tests are weak.

        Maybe something like this?

        100000
a a a a a a a ... aaaa

        (99999 single a's and then 1 word of as a's as possible)

        for (int t = 0; t < n; t++) {
     while (term > 0)
            terminal[term] = 1, term = st[term].link;
}

        +1

        Maximal link depth equals to number of suffixes = string's length, so on reversed test of Junco_dh your solution will fail (prove: your solution is hacked within this test). Reversed, because firstly, you build big automaton, and then on each iteration you go throw all states of it.

Hi — can someone let me know why my E solution is timing out? — https://codeforces.com/contest/1200/submission/58623121

I imagine it to execute in linear time since all I'm doing is for the current final string and the next word to be processed, I'm hashing the suffixes and prefixes of same lengths and determining the largest length at which the hashes are equal.

This length is the length of the prefix of the next word that I know I can skip since it's already present in my current ans string.

My reasoning is, since I'm looking at every next word's character only once, the time complexity should be linear. Am I wrong here, or is my implementation inefficient?

Any help would be much appreciated!

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Monogon
    7 years ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    Monogon
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    +5

    I believe the problem is in the way you are building the string ans with the string's += operator. According to here, time complexity could be as much as linear in the length of the resulting string, even if you are adding one character on the end at a time. Try push_back instead, which should be constant time amortized.

    → Reply

      »
      »
      rameshaditya
      7 years ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      rameshaditya
      7 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +5

      Hi! Thanks for reading my code, I tried resubmitting after changing the line to push_back yet it still times out — https://codeforces.com/contest/1200/submission/58624588

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        racsosabe
        7 years ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        racsosabe
        7 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +29

        I read your code and I made some changes for it to work.

        1) To avoid TLE, use the ansans global string, don't create a new one inside each call of the function ret since that will make it really slow.

        2) When we are using a modulo and we multiply, we should use the conditional 

        if(val >= MOD) val %= MOD;

        Since the substraction of MODMOD might not be able to get valval in the desired range [0,MOD−1][0,MOD−1].

        3) For the rolling hash, one should use as BB factor a number greater than the maximum possible value of the characters we use: Since you are using ASCII code, the maximum value is ASCII(′z′)+1=122+1=123ASCII(′z′)+1=122+1=123, so B=26B=26 isn't enough. B=257B=257 and B=311B=311 are pretty good options for ASCII chars :D

        4) Your code doesn't print anything when n=1n=1, so I changed the way of computing the answer to: First add A0A0, then for each AiAi with 1≤i<n1≤i<n add the correct substring.

        5) The total length might go up to 106106 so we need pp array to be at least that big.

        Your code with the modifications: 58630674

        → Reply

In C , how to prove that g = gcd(n , m) is the no. of dual walls.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Monogon
    7 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Monogon
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +6

    Denote a wall by its clockwise angle from the 12 o'clock position, out of the full 360∘360∘. So the inner walls occur at 0n,1n,…,n−1n0n,1n,…,n−1n and the outer walls occur at 0m,…,m−1m0m,…,m−1m. An inner wall and outer wall coincide when there is a pair (x,y)(x,y) such that 0≤x<n, 0≤y<m0≤x<n, 0≤y<m, and xn=ymxn=ym. That is, xm=ynxm=yn. So, xm=ynxm=yn is a common multiple of mm and nn, so it is divisible by lcm(m,n)=mngcd(m,n)lcm(m,n)=mngcd(m,n). So we write

    xm=yn=kmngcd(m,n),xm=yn=kmngcd(m,n),

    and simplify to

    x=kngcd(m,n),y=kmgcd(m,n).x=kngcd(m,n),y=kmgcd(m,n).

    The restrictions $0\le x<n,\ 0\le y<m$ require that 0≤k<gcd(m,n)0≤k<gcd(m,n). The dual walls correspond exactly with each integer kk in this range, therefore the number of dual walls is gcd(m,n).gcd(m,n).

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      ace_loves_xq
      7 years ago, show #

      7 years ago, hide # ^ |

Thanks for good contest, but personally for me editorial for E was useless and misleading, can't make head or tail of it... What is the purpose of taking min(z, y) if z is smaller than y anyway? Also the whole idea of authors solution seems very unclear to me, especially using prefix function(I got AC with z-function). I don't say that the solution is wrong ot smth but would really appretiate some alternative description of it)

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +10

    Basically, suppose that you have currently formed the string ansans and you will add the string wordword. Then, to minimize the letters added, we need the longest string such that is a suffix of ansans and also a prefix of wordword (we won't need to add this string since it's already formed in ansans). Fortunately, prefix function computes exactly that if we use wordword as pattern and ansans as text (so the answer would be π[|ans|−1]π[|ans|−1] with ππ applied to the text).

    However, this method would be a TLE since ansans might be very long, so we use the following optimization: Since the string wanted is a prefix of wordword, then it's size is at most |word||word|. Thus, we just need to use the last min(|ans|,|word|)min(|ans|,|word|) characters of ansans to use as the text for the prefix function (since any more characters would be unused in the possible prefix).

    We finally end up with a complexity of O(∑i=1n|wordi|)O(∑i=1n|wordi|) since each run of prefix function is linear respect to the length of wordword.

    I hope this helped :D

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    orochimaru001
    7 years ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    orochimaru001
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    can u please explain how u have solved D que??

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      racsosabe
      7 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      racsosabe
      7 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +3

      For problem D one can define a bad row or column as a segment that has at least one Black cell. Now, if we can compute how many bad segments turn all white when painting the square with upper left cell in (i,j)(i,j) in efficient time we can solve the problem iterating over all the valid cells.

      Now, we must notice that if we are handling with a horizontal line, we need to paint the leftmost and the rightmost black cell to make it all white. Thus, if our row is rr and the indices of the former black cells are LL and RR respectively, we see that if we want to make the bad line white we must choose a (i,j)(i,j) in the rectangle defined by the points (minX,minY)(minX,minY) and (maxX,maxY)(maxX,maxY), if and only if the distance between LL and RR is at most kk. Since it will be a complete rectangle of cells to which we have to add 1, we can use the prefix sums technique for 2 dimensions, we will do:

      ac[minX][minY] += 1;
ac[maxX+1][maxY+1] += 1;
ac[minX][maxY+1] -= 1;
ac[maxX+1][minY] -= 1;

      Now we can add up all the bad lines that will become white for each chosen cell using Inclusion-Exclusion Principle (check Max 2D Range Sum Preprocess for a better idea :D).

      But the most important question is: What are minX,minY,maxX,maxYminX,minY,maxX,maxY? To answer this, we need to check that since any pair (x,y)(x,y) with minX≤x≤maxXminX≤x≤maxX and minY≤y≤maxYminY≤y≤maxY paints the bad line completely, it must hold that:

      y≤L∧y+k−1≥Ry≤L∧y+k−1≥R

      Thus, R−k+1≤y≤LR−k+1≤y≤L and following a similar idea we get that r−k+1≤x≤rr−k+1≤x≤r.

      Note: remember that also xx and yy are non-negative.

      We finally have:

      minX=max(0,r−k+1)minX=max(0,r−k+1)
      minY=max(0,R−k+1)minY=max(0,R−k+1)
      maxX=rmaxX=r
      maxY=LmaxY=L

      You can handle with vertical bad lines in a similar way :D

      Finally, add all the values in acac correctly and maximize the number of bad lines. After that, add the number of already white lines.

      I tried to explain the whole idea. I hope that it helps.

      Complexity: O(n2)O(n2).

      My code with the idea: 58630175

      → Reply

Can anyone explain why these submissions : 58626941, 58626900 did not pass the tests but this one did — 58627018 ? Are small primes that useless?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    leloy
    7 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    leloy
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +7

    The prime must be at least the number of characters we need to take care of. We have letters 'a' to 'z', letters 'A' to 'Z', and numbers '0' to '9' — a total of 62 characters. Thus, it's best to have a prime > 62

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +11

    For rolling hash you need your p1p1 to be greater than the maximum possible integral value of the characters you'll use. Since we have an alphabet of size 6262, your p1p1 must be at least 6262 (considering that you mapped the characters to the range [0,61][0,61]).

    → Reply

why is my solution to E not passing TL? 58613989 I did it the same complexity as the editorial but the string i was running kmp on is simply the string in the editorial reversed.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Monogon
    7 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    Monogon
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    The culprit is ans = ans + s[i][j]; Concatenation and string copying takes linear time, and you do this operation for each character, making your solution quadratic. Try ans.push_back(s[i][j]); instead, which is constant amortized.

Can someone help me? Why is this solution getting TL, problem E: https://codeforces.com/contest/1200/submission/58670102.

I have ans — it's a result string.

I use prefix-function to find the length of biggest prefix for each string s.

The parameter for prefix-function is (s + "#" + ans).

For example: if we have "abcdef" and "cdef"

The parameter will be "cdef#abcdef". The biggest prefix is "cdef", size — 4.

So i will add "", to the answer.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    racsosabe
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You need to, optimize the string you use for your prefix function, since ans can be really long. Your just need to take the last min(|ans|,|s|)min(|ans|,|s|) characters of ansans since your common string cannot exceed that length. Also try not to use too much the ++ operator for strings, it's faster to push_back the characters :D

Can anyone please pin down code solution for Div2D following the editorial explanation?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    djm03178
    7 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    djm03178
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Here's one of the correct solutions.

    code
    #include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

const int MAX_N = 2000;

string s[MAX_N], t[MAX_N];

int row_first[MAX_N], row_last[MAX_N];
int col_first[MAX_N], col_last[MAX_N];

int row_ans[MAX_N][MAX_N], col_ans[MAX_N][MAX_N];
int base = 0;
int n, k;

void find_first_last(int *first, int *last, string *board)
{
	int i, j;
	for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
	{
		for (j = 0; j < n; j++)
			if (board[i][j] == 'B')
				first[i] = min(first[i], j), last[i] = max(last[i], j);
		if (last[i] == -1)
			base++;
	}
}

void solve(int *first, int *last, int ans[][MAX_N])
{
	int cur, i, j;
	for (i = 0; i < n - k + 1; i++)
	{
		cur = 0;
		for (j = 0; j < k; j++)
			if (last[j] != -1 && i <= first[j] && last[j] <= i + k - 1)
				cur++;
		ans[0][i] = cur;
		for (; j < n; j++)
		{
			if (last[j] != -1 && i <= first[j] && last[j] <= i + k - 1)
				cur++;
			if (last[j - k] != -1 && i <= first[j - k] && last[j - k] <= i + k - 1)
				cur--;
			ans[j - k + 1][i] = cur;
		}
	}
}

int main()
{
	ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
	cin.tie(0);

	int i, j;
	cin >> n >> k;
	for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
		cin >> s[i];
	for (i = 0; i < n; i++)
		for (j = 0; j < n; j++)
			t[i] += s[j][i];

	fill(row_first, row_first + MAX_N, MAX_N);
	fill(row_last, row_last + MAX_N, -1);
	fill(col_first, col_first + MAX_N, MAX_N);
	fill(col_last, col_last + MAX_N, -1);

	find_first_last(row_first, row_last, s);
	find_first_last(col_first, col_last, t);

	solve(row_first, row_last, row_ans);
	solve(col_first, col_last, col_ans);

	int ans = 0;
	for (i = 0; i < n - k + 1; i++)
		for (j = 0; j < n - k + 1; j++)
			ans = max(ans, row_ans[i][j] + col_ans[j][i]);
	cout << ans + base << '\n';
}
    → Reply
»

Hi,can someone explain why we are using 2520 states for problem F in detail?

I initially thought of defining a state using two variables using vertex v and value c (v,c) but c is very large leading to large number of total states. I wouldbe thankful if someone explained why we are taking 2520 states for each vertex v.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    bruno.iljazovic
    7 years ago, show #
    »
    »
    bruno.iljazovic
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    2520 is the lowest common multiple of 1,2,...,10 — all possible numbers of outgoing edges.

    So (v, 2521) is essentially the same state as (v, 1) because they have the same remainder modulo 1,2,...,10

    → Reply
»
2022OVO
7 years ago, show (+3) #

For problem F, can someone explain how is the LCM term coming? (Specifically, how is the number of states for a particular vertex 2520 and the logic behind decomposition of the graph in various states).

I'm not sure what the author means by state here.

→ Reply

    • »
      »

      Just__a__Visitor
      7 years ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      Just__a__Visitor
      7 years ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 3 →  
      0

      I still have some confusion. Suppose you visit any vertex V. Now, after adding the integer written at that vertex to c, the next vertex would be the one present at the index c%m_i. Since, c%m_i can take atmost 10 distinct values, we can infer that the state of a vertex can be defined by the vertex number and c%m_i (which is almost 10), So any vertex can have almost 10 different states (as we can determine the next vertex just by looking at c)

      This was my initial thought process but I'm not quite sure where I went wrong. Can you please elaborate on this.

      Another thing that I couldn't understand is the role of LCM. (Like, why didn't we just multiply all the numbers from 1 to 10, or maybe add them). What is the role of LCM in this case.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        djm03178
        7 years ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        djm03178
        7 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +1

        Each of them has indeed at most 10 outgoing edges, but you shouldn't consider them same only by their remainders.

        Suppose that there are two vertices 1 and 2. Vertex 1 has 2 outgoing edges and vertex 2 has 3 outgoing edges and both of their k values are 0. If you only consider the remainder for the states, you'll say that (1,0) is a same state as (1,2). But let's assume that e_1[0] is 2, then you see both (1,0) and (1,2) will go to vertex 2. However, (2,0) and (2,2) are different states and will use different outgoing edges. That's why you should consider (1,0) and (1,2) differently, even though 0 % 2 == 2 % 2.

        Now about the role of LCM: You can think of it similar to problem C. Consider that you have infinite number of rulers of same length, and put them next to each other horizontally, starting from position 0. Then do it again for another set of rulers of different length. Which position is the first position that the rulers' end meet at? It's their LCM. That means, the entire graph has a common cycle of c of length LCM(1..10). Of course, 10! also works, but this only increases the number of states to 3628800 * n so it's infeasible to consider all of the states (and this was one of the solutions that I intended to prevent from passing). 1+2+...+10 doesn't work, though.

        → Reply

How is the time complexity calculated in problem F? Shouldn't it depend on the number of loops in the graph?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    djm03178
    7 years ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    djm03178
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Every state is visited at most once. If you're about to re-visit a particular state, then that means you already know the answer for that state (the loop that it'll end on) — so you can just use it without re-visiting it.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      gauss14
      7 years ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      gauss14
      7 years ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      But if there are many loops?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        djm03178
        7 years ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        djm03178
        7 years ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +3

        It doesn't matter. Each state ends in exactly one loop, and no two loops share a same state. No matter how many loops there are, you only need to check at most 2520n states.

        → Reply

https://codeforces.com/contest/1200/submission/58692995: Isn't this solution O(N*N*2520 + Q) ? How did it not TLE ? djm03178, is this the timestamp method being referred to, in the editorial ?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    djm03178
    7 years ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    djm03178
    7 years ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Well it's not that kind of timestamp I mentioned. What I referred to is that you use different timestamp for different travels. This way you can look through all the vertices you visited in the loop (i.e. you can backtrack what states you have visited), and only increase the answer if the visited time is not same as this travel, and update the timestamp for that vertex.

    Anyways the time complexity for that submission is actually O(min(2520n, q)*n), since it requires at most one run of the loop per query. It should be fast enough since the loop barely does any work.

    Maybe I could make a case where that makes a lot of cache misses, but I don't know.

    → Reply
